Docetaxel administration - N (%) | Treat.reasoning: On page 5, under 'OVERALL SURVIVAL', it states: 'Early docetaxel treatment was planned in 45% of the patients... but was not administered to 22 patients, 11 in each of the randomly assigned treatment groups.' Table 1 on page 4 shows that 254 patients (45%) in the Enzalutamide group planned early docetaxel. Subtracting the 11 patients who did not receive it (254 - 11 = 243) gives the number who received docetaxel. The total N for the Enzalutamide group is 563 (from Table 1), so the percentage is (243/563) * 100 = 43.16%, rounded to 43%.

Docetaxel administration - N (%) | Contr.reasoning: On page 5, under 'OVERALL SURVIVAL', it states: 'Early docetaxel treatment was planned in 45% of the patients... but was not administered to 22 patients, 11 in each of the randomly assigned treatment groups.' Table 1 on page 4 shows that 249 patients (44%) in the Standard Care group planned early docetaxel. Subtracting the 11 patients who did not receive it (249 - 11 = 238) gives the number who received docetaxel. The total N for the Standard Care group is 562 (from Table 1), so the percentage is (238/562) * 100 = 42.35%, rounded to 42%.

Treatment Class.value: Enzalutamide (an androgen-receptor inhibitor) and Standard nonsteroidal antiandrogen therapy (e.g., bicalutamide, nilutamide, or flutamide). Both treatment classes were administered with testosterone suppression and optionally early docetaxel.

Type of Therapy.value: Participants were assigned to receive testosterone suppression plus either open-label enzalutamide (an androgen-receptor inhibitor at a dose of 160 mg daily) or a standard nonsteroidal antiandrogen therapy (bicalutamide, nilutamide, or flutamide). Early administration of docetaxel (75 mg per square meter of body-surface area, without prednisone or prednisolone, given every 3 weeks for a maximum of six cycles) with testosterone suppression was also permitted as a stratification factor.
Type of Therapy.reasoning: The 'METHODS' section on page 1 states: 'In this open-label, randomized, phase 3 trial, we assigned patients to receive testosterone suppression plus either open-label enzalutamide or a standard nonsteroidal antiandrogen therapy (standard-care group).' The 'PATIENTS AND TREATMENT' section on page 2 further elaborates: 'Eligible patients were randomly assigned in a 1:1 ratio to receive enzalutamide (at a dose of 160 mg daily) or a standard nonsteroidal antiandrogen drug (bicalutamide, nilutamide, or flutamide) (standard-care group) until the occurrence of clinical disease progression or prohibitive toxic effects. In the two groups, background therapy included continuous testosterone suppression.' It also mentions: 'the early administration of docetaxel with testosterone suppression was permitted in protocol version 2... If docetaxel was administered, the regimen consisted of 75 mg per square meter of body-surface area, without prednisone or prednisolone, given every 3 weeks for a maximum of six cycles.'
